a: The estimate excludes physical machine automation and concerns only distributor labor tasks.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Construction and Mining (except Oil Well) Machinery and Equipment Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: High-value new and used equipment and recurring parts transactions supported by OEM territories, stocked inventory, application expertise, field sales, delivery, warranty coordination, financing facilitation, and installed-base support; revenue is transactional and cyclical rather than subscription-based.
lens.excluded_activities: OEM captive branches, oil-well equipment wholesalers, pure rental fleets, repair-only contractors, manufacturers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles; ancillary dealer repair and rental are considered only as context for the transferable distribution relationship, not as the screened service basket.
lens.included_activities: Independent and owner-controlled merchant wholesalers repeatedly supplying construction, roadbuilding, mining, quarrying, logging, forestry, and related machinery, work tools, and replacement parts to external contractors, producers, governments, and equipment users.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader NAICS 423 durable-goods wholesale occupation mix, led by wholesale sales representatives, material movers, shipping/receiving clerks, technical sales representatives, and delivery drivers, rather than six-digit wage-weighted tasks. I assign partial AI exposure to quoting, lead and account research, parts identification, order entry, inventory and demand analysis, warranty documents, credit, and administration, while treating physical logistics, site assessment, relationship selling, and accountable equipment recommendations as less exposed. The proxy may overstate exposure because this industry's sales are highly technical and field-based, or understate it if parts and installed-base data work is unusually large.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for all durable-goods wholesalers, not 423810.
Public OEM and dealer technology may exceed LMM practice.
The estimate excludes physical machine automation and concerns only distributor labor tasks.
a.change_evidence: A 423810-specific OEWS occupation-by-wage extract combined with task logs from equipment and parts sales, product support, inventory, warranty, warehouse, delivery, credit, and finance teams, with current dealer-system automation identified, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Equipment distribution has meaningful information work but a large physical and relationship component. Alta's equipment-dealership ERP tracks inventory, labor, service history, and operational data, and Caterpillar invests in digital dealer solutions and data analytics, showing a usable data foundation. The not-yet-automated opportunity remains below one-half because expensive equipment decisions and logistics need people and assets.

d5.bridge: Construction spending and BLS end-market employment projections proxy equipment and parts quantity rather than measuring it. BLS projects 2024-34 construction employment up 4.4% but mining, quarrying, and oil and gas extraction down 1.6%; 2025 Census data show private construction down 2.9% and public construction up 3.6%. Alta reported cyclical softness but public infrastructure as a counterbalance. I translate this mixed evidence into roughly flat-to-modest five-year real basket growth with a wide cyclical range. The proxy can overstate demand if fleet utilization, used equipment, or rental substitutes for purchases and understate it if infrastructure, reshoring, mining capex, and aging fleets accelerate.
caveats: Construction spending is nominal and not equipment quantity.
BLS employment is an imperfect proxy for capital equipment demand and the mining series includes oil and gas excluded from this code.
Year-five results are highly sensitive to the point in the equipment replacement cycle.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year AED and OEM dealer retail unit, parts, fleet age, utilization, and inventory data by construction, mining, quarry, and forestry segment, combined with public infrastructure obligation schedules and private construction forecasts, would materially move the range.
d5.rationale: Roads, infrastructure, buildings, aggregates, mining, forestry, and maintenance require physical machinery and replacement parts. Parts recur with the installed base even when new-equipment purchases pause. High interest rates, private-construction weakness, commodity cycles, tariffs, dealer inventory corrections, and equipment life extension make year-five demand uncertain and cyclical.

caveats: The frozen count is margin-bridged and may include firms outside the true EBITDA band.
Reported company activity often spans wholesale, retail, rental, repair, and multiple NAICS codes.
A distributor without transferable OEM rights may not be an eligible operating platform.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level classification of the frozen 793 candidates by independent ownership, external-customer revenue, equipment-versus-parts mix, rental and repair share, OEM agreement assignability, repeat-customer share, branch assets, and owner/key-salesperson dependence would tighten eligibility.
e.rationale: The code consists of merchant distribution of equipment and related parts, and dealer economics ordinarily include repeat installed-base needs. Many independent LMM firms therefore meet the lens. The eligible share is reduced for captive OEM branches, companies dominated by rental or repair outside the code's merchant activity, brokers without durable operating capabilities, businesses dependent on a personal selling relationship, and dealerships whose territory or diagnostic rights will not transfer.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423810 ($10,781,857,764) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423810 ($104,852,814,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,036,669 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423810); class <5: 1,132 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,042,572 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $232,240 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 414 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,249,882 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $596,912 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 354 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,073,282 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,145,332 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 439 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,118,866 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,538,215 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 150 firms, avg revenue/firm $131,227,987 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,920,622 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 111 firms, avg revenue/firm $571,674,315 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,999,370 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 793

o.bridge: Alta and Caterpillar describe independent dealer territories, OEM parts, diagnostic software, digital customer/dealer solutions, inventory, and installed-base support, but do not measure the year-five share requiring a merchant operator. I infer that digital tools change discovery and workflow while physical machines, parts, warranties, OEM authorization, credit, trade-ins, and delivery preserve operator accountability. This can overstate persistence if OEM direct sales and online parts gain share, and understate it where exclusive territory and diagnostic access make dealers indispensable.
caveats: Operator-required quantity differs from human-touch frequency.
OEMs control territories, diagnostic systems, and parts access and could reshape the channel.
Used equipment marketplaces can self-serve more of the transaction while still relying on physical fulfillment.
o.change_evidence: OEM channel-strategy interviews, dealer agreement reviews, buyer surveys, direct-sales and online-parts share, drop-ship rates, and order data by channel and product class would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software cannot take ownership of bulky inventory, demonstrate and configure machines, coordinate financing and trade-ins, deliver equipment and urgent parts, administer warranty, or hold OEM territory rights. Some standardized parts and lead-generation work can move direct or self-service, but most quantity still requires an accountable equipment distributor.

caveats: Alta's 2025 equipment gross margin compression shows cycle and inventory can overwhelm operational gains.
Exclusive territories protect some lines but not used equipment or cross-brand competition.
No source directly measures AI benefit retention in LMM dealers.
q.change_evidence: Matched branch and account data before and after automation covering gross margin by equipment and parts, SG&A hours, quote conversion, discounting, inventory aging, churn, and OEM rebate realization over several buying cycles would measure retained benefit.
q.rationale: Automation benefits can be retained through lower administrative hiring, faster parts and equipment quotes, better inventory turns, fewer warranty errors, stronger lead coverage, and improved price discipline. Territorial rights, product expertise, uptime, and installed-base knowledge create some insulation. Retention is reduced by competitive equipment bidding, OEM pricing and rebates, public procurement, sophisticated contractors, cyclical discounting, and customer expectation that productivity improves service or price.

rho.bridge: Alta's dealership-specific ERP and Caterpillar's digital dealer investments demonstrate same-channel data and workflow feasibility but do not measure LMM five-year implementation of exposed tasks. I discount for fragmented dealer systems, OEM data rights, legacy part masters, cybersecurity, low transaction frequency, change capacity, and costly recommendation errors. The large-company proxy likely biases achievable implementation upward.
caveats: ERP availability is not evidence of AI labor realization.
OEM-controlled software and territories can limit a buyer's freedom to standardize systems.
LMM firms may lack clean serial-number, inventory, and customer histories.
rho.change_evidence: A multi-dealer study measuring sustained adoption, labor hours, quote cycle time, parts-match accuracy, forecast error, warranty acceptance, and override rates for AI-supported workflows over 24 months would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI assistance is feasible for catalog and serial-number search, quote drafting, lead prioritization, inventory forecasts, warranty packages, collections, and administrative processing. Implementation will be slower where OEM systems restrict access, transactions are bespoke, data are sparse, or outputs affect safety, uptime, credit, or high-value asset decisions.

s5.bridge: A national owner-readiness report says 49% of owners wanted to exit within five years, and Census-based reporting says 51% of small-business owners were over 55; neither measures completed 423810 control transfers. I roughly halve exit intent for delay, internal succession, closure, failed processes, and OEM rejection. Alta reports 17 acquisitions since 2020 and says dealership owners frequently approach it, confirming an active same-channel buyer path, but its pace may overrepresent larger or strategically attractive dealerships. Broad intentions likely overstate actual completed transfers.
caveats: National surveys measure intent or age rather than qualifying transfers.
Alta acquisitions include adjacent material-handling and other equipment verticals.
OEM approval can make seller intent irrelevant.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction counts from AED industry brokers, BizBuySell, GF Data, Axial, DealStats, OEM dealer-development teams, and strategic buyers for U.S. 423810 firms with $1-10M normalized EBITDA, classified by control and seller outcome, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Owner aging, OEM preference for fewer financially stable dealers, and strategic consolidators create real transfer flow. Completion remains constrained by manufacturer consent, territory overlap, cyclic earnings, inventory and floorplan exposure, key technicians and salespeople, and the limited buyer pool for a capital-intensive dealership.

principal_driver: The installed equipment base produces recurring, uptime-sensitive parts demand protected in many markets by OEM territory and diagnostic rights.
principal_weakness: OEM control, inventory capital, and construction/mining cycles can impair both transferability and near-term economics despite operational improvements.
